nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving streams and river tributaries with abundant driftwood, submerged branches, and leaf litter. The species is strongly associated with woody debris and reed beds, where its twig-like body shape provides effective camouflage against predators.

decorPreferences: driftwood and submerged branches (essential — resting and grazing surface)
dense planting to provide cover and additional grazing surfaces

diet.notes: The Farlowella is a continuous grazer that rasps biofilm, algae, and microorganisms from wood and rock surfaces throughout the day. It requires a mature, well-established tank with a healthy growth of algae and biofilm on the hardscape; in a newly set-up or sterile aquarium lacking sufficient biofilm, specimens tend to decline even when algae wafers and vegetables are offered, as they are poorly adapted to picking up food from the substrate. Supplement natural grazing with algae wafers, spirulina wafers, blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, or spinach, and sinking herbivore pellets. Farlowella will also graze the surface of soft driftwood. A diet heavy in protein-rich foods is not recommended and has been associated with digestive issues.

goodWith: small plecos that occupy different grazing zones
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that cause stress
fish competing directly for algae grazing zones in small tanks
high-flow setups with inadequate calm areas

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Farlowella acus showing characteristic elongated twig-like body shape.

summary: The Farlowella, also called the Twig Catfish, is a slender catfish (Farlowella acus) from Venezuela and the Orinoco basin that mimics a drifting stick, reaching around 20 cm. It grazes constantly on biofilm and algae and requires a mature, well-established tank to thrive. Peaceful and sensitive to water quality. Many trade specimens belong to related species in the genus.
careNotes:
The Farlowella is one of the most distinctive catfishes available in the aquarium hobby, with a hyper-elongated snout and body that closely mimics a drifting twig or plant stem — a camouflage strategy that makes it nearly invisible among submerged wood and leaf litter. Specimens sold in the trade as Farlowella acus frequently represent other species within the Farlowella genus complex, as reliable species-level identification requires specialist morphological assessment; all species in the genus share essentially identical aquarium care requirements.

The single most critical husbandry requirement is a mature tank with well-established biofilm and algal growth on driftwood and hardscape. Farlowella are constant, low-intensity grazers that spend most of their day slowly moving across surfaces rasping aufwuchs (the thin layer of algae, bacteria, and microorganisms that coats submerged surfaces in established aquariums). In a tank lacking sufficient biofilm — particularly a newly set-up aquarium — they will slowly starve even when algae wafers and vegetables are offered, as they are poorly adapted to picking up food from the substrate. A tank of at least six months’ maturity is strongly recommended before introducing this species, much as it is for Otocinclus catfish.

Water quality must be pristine with low nitrates (ideally under 20 ppm) and stable, soft, slightly acidic to neutral conditions. Hard or alkaline water is not well tolerated. Moderate to low flow suits them best; they are not strong swimmers and high turbulence can be stressful. Temperature should remain stable, as sudden drops are poorly tolerated.

Farlowella are shy and largely nocturnal in activity, spending daytime hours clinging motionlessly to driftwood or plant stems. They are best kept with calm, slow-moving tankmates and are poor competitors at feeding time; algae wafers and blanched vegetables should be placed directly near their resting areas to ensure they receive enough food. Driftwood is essentially mandatory — it provides both a resting surface and a supplementary food source, as the species grazes its surface biofilm and the ingested cellulose is thought to aid digestion.
breedingNotes: Farlowella breed in captivity with moderate frequency when conditions are suitable. The male guards and fans a clutch of eggs deposited on a flat, clean surface — often the aquarium glass, a broad leaf, or smooth slate. Eggs hatch in approximately 7–10 days, with the male providing all parental care until the fry are free-swimming. Fry resemble miniature adults and require the same biofilm-rich diet; blanched zucchini slices placed near them are commonly recommended as an early food alongside natural biofilm growth. Breeding is more reliably triggered in soft, slightly acidic, well-oxygenated water. Moving the breeding pair to a dedicated species tank is generally considered to improve success rates significantly.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Farlowella acus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, water parameters (temperature 23–27°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm/~2–12 dGH), native range (Venezuela, Colombia, Trinidad), habitat (slow streams with woody debris), diet (aufwuchs/biofilm), tank requirements, breeding behaviour, and conspecific/interspecific compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Farlowella acus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 20.6 cm TL), native range (Orinoco basin, Venezuela), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), and diet classification (herbivore/aufwuchs). Confirmed temperature range 23–27°C.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Twig Catfish (Farlowella)
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide confirming mature-tank requirement analogous to Otocinclus, aufwuchs grazing behaviour, driftwood necessity, soft slightly acidic water preference, peaceful temperament, and breeding on flat surfaces with paternal egg guarding.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Whiptail and Twig Catfishes
additional.notes: Provided context on the Farlowella genus complex and the frequent misidentification of trade specimens; confirmed ~20 cm maximum size, Venezuela and Orinoco drainage origin, camouflage strategy as twig/stick mimic, intermediate care level due to water quality and algae demands.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Loricariid Catfishes of the Orinoco
additional.notes: Background on Loricariidae diversity and Farlowella natural habitat in Venezuelan Orinoco tributaries; confirmed association with submerged woody debris, slow currents, and biofilm-rich aufwuchs feeding strategy.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard and fan egg clutches deposited on flat surfaces such as aquarium glass, slate, or broad leaves. Eggs hatch in approximately 7–10 days, with the male providing sole parental care until the fry are free-swimming. Fry require abundant biofilm and benefit from blanched zucchini as an early supplemental food. Moving the pair to a dedicated species tank is generally considered to improve success rates significantly.
